title: Practising Your Argument Writing Skills
explanation_chunks: Now that you understand what a balanced argument is, it's your turn to create one! You'll work in groups to pick a topic, like whether homework is necessary, and brainstorm ideas.
For your chosen topic, write down all the points that support it and all the points that go against it. Think about different angles, just like we did with the computer games and zoo arguments.

Organize your ideas logically, putting the strongest points first. Then, add special 'connective' words like 'however,' 'therefore,' or 'in addition.' These words are like signposts that guide your reader smoothly from one idea to the next.

Don't forget to write a clear introduction to set the stage and a strong conclusion that ties everything back together. Imagine you're talking to your friends, then imagine you're talking to your teachers or parents – how would your words change? This helps you think about your audience.

Finally, always proofread your work! Check for correct spelling, punctuation, and clear sentences. Using commas after intro phrases and semicolons or colons to link ideas will make your writing shine.
examples: Topics to discuss: 'Should children receive pocket money?' or 'Should learning a new language be compulsory?'
Using a connective: 'Many adults believe homework is essential. However, many children disagree.' 'However' connects the two opposing views.
Changing language for audience: You might say 'It's super important!' to a friend, but 'It is of great importance' to an assembly of parents.
key_points: Choose a clear topic for your argument.
Brainstorm points both for and against the topic.
Organize points logically, from strongest to weakest.
Use connectives (like 'However', 'Therefore', 'In addition') to link ideas smoothly.
Write a clear introduction and a conclusion that refers back to it.
Adapt your language depending on who your audience is.
Always proofread your work for spelling, punctuation, and grammar.
Pictures can visually support your written arguments.

vocabulary.definition: Words or phrases that link clauses, sentences, or paragraphs together, helping the text flow smoothly (e.g., 'however', 'therefore', 'in addition').
Required by law or rule; something you must do.
A question asked for effect or to make a point, rather than to get a real answer.
To read text and mark any errors in spelling, grammar, or punctuation.
A title or brief explanation accompanying an illustration, photograph, or diagram.

explain_again: This page is all about you becoming an argument writer! You pick a topic, think of all the good things and bad things about it, and then write them down in a super clear way. Remember to use special 'connecting' words to make your ideas stick together, and always read it over to fix any mistakes. It's like building a strong bridge of words!
fun_analogy: Writing your own argument is like building a LEGO castle. You collect all the right bricks (your ideas for and against), then carefully put them together in the right order (your plan), using special connecting pieces (your connectives) to make sure it's strong and makes sense. And once it's built, you show it off and make sure every brick is perfectly in place!
